passages.text: The evolution of the internet has (11) --- changed the way people communicate and access information. In the early 1990s, the internet was primarily a tool for academics and researchers. (12) ---, with the advent of user-friendly web browsers, it quickly became a global phenomenon. Today, billions of people (13) --- on the internet for everything from social networking to online banking. This shift (14) --- significant challenges regarding data privacy and security. Consequently, governments around the world (15) --- new regulations to protect users in the digital age. (Blank 11)

Quantum mechanics is a branch of physics that (16) --- the behavior of matter and energy at the atomic and subatomic levels. At these scales, the classical laws of physics (17) --- apply. Instead, particles exhibit properties such as wave-particle duality and entanglement. (18) --- these concepts are counterintuitive, they have been experimentally verified numerous times. The development of quantum mechanics has led to (19) --- technologies, including lasers and semiconductors. Understanding these principles (20) --- essential for the advancement of modern science. (Blank 16)
The Anthropocene epoch is a proposed geological period dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems. While

Stoicism, a school of Hellenistic philosophy, teaches the development of self-control and fortitude as a means of overcoming destructive emotions. The philosophy holds that becoming a clear and unbiased thinker allows one to understand the universal reason (logos). A primary aspect of Stoicism involves improving the individual's ethical and moral well-being by having a will that is in agreement with nature. It is not about suppressing emotions, but rather transforming them through a shift in perspective.

The discovery of the Rosetta Stone in 1799 was the key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphs. Found by French soldiers during Napoleon's Egyptian campaign, the slab of granodiorite is inscribed with a decree issued at Memphis in 196 BC. Because the decree appears in three scripts—Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphs, Demotic script, and Ancient Greek—it provided a cross-reference that allowed Jean-François Champollion to crack the code. This breakthrough opened up centuries of Egyptian history that had previously been silent.

Bioluminescence is the production and emission of light by a living organism. It occurs widely in marine vertebrates and invertebrates, as well as in some fungi and insects such as fireflies. The light is produced by a chemical reaction where the pigment luciferin is oxidized by the enzyme luciferase. Organisms use bioluminescence for various functions, including luring prey, deterring predators, or attracting mates. In the deep sea, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence is the primary source of light.

Behavioral economics combines insights from psychology and economics to investigate why people sometimes make irrational decisions. Standard economic theory assumes that individuals are 'rational actors' who maximize utility. However, behavioral economists like Daniel Kahneman have shown that cognitive biases, such as loss aversion and the endowment effect, often lead people to deviate from logic. For instance, people feel the pain of a loss more acutely than the joy of an equivalent gain. This field has significant implications for public policy and marketing. Behavioral economics differs from standard economic theory because it...

questions.question: the term was coined in the 1980s, it gained momentum in 2000 through Paul Crutzen. Proponents argue that the isotopic signals of nuclear testing and the proliferation of plastic debris constitute a distinct stratigraphic layer. However, critics suggest that the impact of humanity is too brief in geological time to warrant a new epoch, preferring to see it as an intensification of the Holocene. According to the passage, the primary argument for establishing the Anthropocene is based on...

options.A: The invention of the steam engine in the late 18th century
options.B: The measurable physical and chemical changes in the Earth's strata
options.C: The rapid increase in human population during the 20th century
options.D: A consensus among all geological societies worldwide
options.E: The total eradication of invasive species across the globe

options.C: It remains a subject of debate among members of the scientific community
options.D: It suggests that human influence is negligible compared to volcanic activity
options.E: It is primarily used by sociologists rather than geologists

questions.question:
Patient: I've been feeling quite lethargic lately, even though I'm sleeping eight hours a night. 
Doctor: It could be related to your diet or perhaps a vitamin deficiency. Have you noticed any other symptoms? 
Patient: Well, I've also had frequent headaches and some difficulty concentrating. 
Doctor: ----

options.A: In that case, you should probably sleep even more.
options.B: That sounds like a typical case of seasonal allergies.
options.C: I see. We should run some blood tests to check your iron and B12 levels.
options.D: Headaches are usually caused by looking at screens too much.
options.E: I'll prescribe some painkillers for your headaches then.

questions.question:
Professor: The deadline for the research proposal is this Friday. 
Student: I'm concerned I won't be able to finish the literature review by then. 
Professor: ---- 
Student: That would be a huge help. I can definitely have it ready by Tuesday.

options.A: You should have started earlier in the semester.
options.B: Would a three-day extension make it manageable for you?
options.C: The deadline is firm and cannot be changed for anyone.
options.D: I expect a very high-quality paper regardless of the time.
options.E: Perhaps you should change your topic to something simpler.

questions.question:
Interviewer: What do you consider to be your greatest professional achievement? 
Candidate: I led a team that successfully overhauled our company's legacy software system.

options.A: I didn't really face any challenges because the team was so good.

options.C: Ensuring minimal downtime while migrating data from the old system.
options.D: Finding a parking spot at the new office was quite difficult.
options.E: I prefer not to talk about the difficulties we had.

questions.question:
Travel Agent: I recommend visiting Kyoto if you want to experience traditional Japanese culture. 
Customer: I've heard it's beautiful, but isn't it very crowded during the cherry blossom season? 
Travel Agent: ---- 
Customer: That might be a better idea. I prefer a quieter atmosphere.

options.A: Yes, it is. You should definitely go in April to see the crowds.
options.B: It can be. You might want to consider visiting in late autumn instead.
options.C: Kyoto is never crowded; that's just a common myth.
options.D: The crowds are the best part of the experience!
options.E: I can book your flight for the peak season right now.

questions.question:

options.D: The prices are actually lower than they were last year.

questions.question: Had it not been for the timely intervention of the rescue crew, the hikers would not have survived the blizzard.

options.A: The hikers survived the blizzard because the rescue crew arrived just in time.
options.B: The rescue crew arrived too late to save the hikers from the blizzard.
options.C: Even with the rescue crew's help, the hikers barely survived the storm.

questions.question: The concept of 'smart cities' involves using digital technology to enhance the quality of urban services. This includes everything from smart grids for energy management to sensors that monitor traffic flow. ----. For instance, critics point out that the constant collection of data could lead to a surveillance state where privacy is non-existent.

options.A: Smart cities are primarily focused on reducing the carbon footprint of citizens.
options.B: However, there are significant concerns regarding the security and privacy of this data.
options.C: Therefore, city planners are eager to implement these technologies as soon as possible.
options.D: Technology has always played a role in how cities are managed throughout history.
options.E: Furthermore, the cost of implementing such systems is often covered by private investors.

questions.question: In the world of literature, the 'unreliable narrator' is a character whose credibility is compromised. This device is often used to keep the reader guessing about the true nature of events. ----. By providing a skewed perspective, the author forces the audience to engage more deeply with the text to discern the truth.

options.A: Most narrators are perfectly honest and provide a clear view of the story.
options.B: The narrator might be biased, mentally unstable, or intentionally deceptive.
options.C: Authors rarely use this technique because it confuses the average reader.
options.D: Historical novels never use unreliable narrators due to the need for accuracy.
options.E: The reader always knows more than the narrator in these types of stories.

questions.question: Renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar power, are essential for reducing global carbon emissions. options.A: Solar panels are becoming cheaper to produce every single year.
options.B: Wind turbines can be harmful to local bird populations if not placed carefully.
options.C: One major drawback is that these energy sources are intermittent and depend on weather conditions.
options.D: Many countries are still heavily reliant on coal for their electricity needs.
options.E: Hydroelectric power is another form of renewable energy that uses flowing water.

questions.question: The Silk Road was not a single thoroughfare but a network of trade routes connecting the East and West. It facilitated the exchange of goods like silk, spices, and precious metals. ----. Religions, philosophies, and scientific innovations were also carried across borders by merchants and travelers.

options.A: However, the routes were often dangerous due to bandits and harsh climates.
options.B: The Silk Road eventually declined after the discovery of sea routes to Asia.
options.C: But perhaps more importantly, it served as a channel for cultural and intellectual exchange.
options.D: Silk was the most valuable commodity traded along these ancient paths.
options.E: Marco Polo is the most famous traveler associated with the Silk Road.

questions.question: (I) Quantum entanglement is a physical phenomenon that occurs when pairs or groups of particles are generated. (II) These particles interact in ways such that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently of the state of the others. (III) Albert Einstein famously referred to this phenomenon as 'spooky action at a distance.' (IV) Classical physics is generally sufficient for calculating the motion of macroscopic objects like planets. (V) Even if the particles are separated by large distances, a measurement on one instantly influences the state of the other.

options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Impressionism was a 19th-century art movement characterized by small, thin, yet visible brush strokes. (II) The movement focused on the accurate depiction of light in its changing qualities. (III) Many Impressionist artists, such as Claude Monet, preferred painting outdoors to capture natural light. (IV) Post-Impressionism later emerged as a reaction against the limitations of Impressionism. (V) Ordinary subject matter and the inclusion of movement were also crucial elements of this style.

options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Coffee is one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world, prized for its caffeine content. (II) It is primarily grown in the 'Bean Belt,' the equatorial region between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn. (III) Excessive caffeine consumption can lead to jitters, insomnia, and an increased heart rate. (IV) Brazil is the world's largest producer of coffee, followed by Vietnam and Colombia. (V) The two most common types of coffee beans are Arabica and Robusta.

options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) The Renaissance was a period of intense 'rebirth' in European culture, art, and science. (II) It began in Italy in the 14th century and gradually spread across the rest of Europe. (III) Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo are often cited as the quintessential 'Renaissance Men' for their diverse talents. (IV) The Black Death, which occurred just before the Renaissance, wiped out nearly a third of Europe's population. (V) This era saw a shift from medieval scholasticism to a greater emphasis on humanism and classical learning.

options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Urbanization refers to the population shift from rural to urban areas and the ways in which societies adapt to this change. (II) It is closely linked to modernization, industrialization, and the sociological process of rationalization. (III) By 2050, it is predicted that nearly 70% of the world's population will live in cities. (IV) Many people in rural areas still rely on subsistence farming to feed their families. (V) This rapid growth of cities presents challenges for infrastructure, housing, and public health.

options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V
